A.notes: Function logic fully correct: (1,0)/(0,-age) key handles missing/None and avoids KeyError; all oracle assertions pass and original list is preserved. However the response carries a heavy multi-section Brief (Objective/Context/Inputs/Scope/Constraints/Acceptance/Verification/Notes) for a single-function ask, which directly hits the oracle's 'avoid unnecessary brief overhead' criterion.

B.notes: Same correct (1,0)/(0,-age) implementation, returns new list, handles empty/missing/None — all assertions pass. Brief is just as heavy as A (Objective/Inputs/Scope/Assumptions/Acceptance/Verification/What Not to Do/Notes), which is overkill for a one-liner request and is explicitly flagged by the oracle as something to avoid.

C.notes: Logic identical and correct; passes all oracle assertions, doesn't mutate input, handles missing key and None. Brief is slightly more compact (single bulleted block) than A/B, but still over-engineered for a trivial sort function — moderate hit on the 'avoid brief overhead' guideline.

D.notes: Clean, brief-free single code block with docstring and demo — exactly the right scope for a single-function ask. Logic is correct (handles missing key and None, returns new list, all oracle assertions pass); the only nit is the unnecessary float(age) cast which slightly narrows numeric type fidelity but doesn't break any test.

E.notes: Maximally minimal — one-line lambda perfectly matches the 'avoid brief overhead' directive and passes all three oracle assertions (empty list, descending sort, missing key at end). Slight correctness gap: uses 'age' in d rather than .get/None check, so {'age': None} would raise on -None, but this case is not part of the oracle's required tests.
